basic_info.name: PCM Encapsulation for Thermal Storage
basic_info.description: HSP matching between phase-change materials (PCMs) and shell/matrix polymers is used to predict encapsulation compatibility and long-term cycling stability in thermal energy storage systems. PCMs (paraffins, fatty acids, salt hydrates) undergo solid-liquid transitions to store/release latent heat. Microencapsulation in polymer shells (melamine-formaldehyde, PMMA, polyurea) protects the PCM from leakage. HSP compatibility between the molten PCM core and the shell polymer determines interfacial adhesion, shell integrity under thermal cycling, and resistance to PCM permeation through the shell wall. A moderate HSP distance ensures the shell does not dissolve in or swell excessively with the PCM, while sufficient affinity maintains adhesion. This approach enables rational shell polymer selection and predicts long-term durability of encapsulated PCM systems.

hsp_principle.hsp_principle: The principle relies on controlling the HSP distance between the molten PCM core and the polymeric shell material. If the Ra distance is too small (RED << 1), the shell polymer may swell or dissolve in the PCM, leading to shell failure. If Ra is too large (RED >> 1), poor interfacial adhesion results in delamination and cracking during thermal cycling. An optimal intermediate RED range exists where the shell maintains structural integrity while providing sufficient adhesion to the PCM. For paraffin-based PCMs (dominated by dD), shell polymers with moderate polar and hydrogen bonding components create an appropriate mismatch that prevents dissolution while crosslinked networks resist swelling. The HSP approach also guides selection of surfactants and emulsifiers used during the encapsulation process (e.g., in situ polymerization, coacervation) by matching surfactant HSP to both core and shell phases.
hsp_principle.key_equation: Ra = sqrt(4*(dD_PCM - dD_shell)^2 + (dP_PCM - dP_shell)^2 + (dH_PCM - dH_shell)^2); RED = Ra/R0_shell; Swelling ratio correlates with 1/Ra^2
hsp_principle.typical_red_threshold: RED 0.8-1.2 optimal for encapsulation; RED < 0.6 risk of shell swelling/dissolution; RED > 1.5 poor adhesion and delamination risk

required_inputs: HSP values (dD, dP, dH) of the PCM in liquid state
HSP values (dD, dP, dH) of the shell polymer
Interaction radius R0 of the shell polymer
PCM melting temperature and operating temperature range
data_sources: HSPiP database for common paraffins and polymers
Group contribution methods for PCM HSP estimation (Van Krevelen)
Literature values for paraffin wax HSP (Archer 1991, Hansen 2007)
Experimental solubility/swelling tests for shell polymer sphere fitting
Supplier datasheets for commercial PCM products
complementary_parameters: PCM melting point and latent heat of fusion
Shell polymer Tg and crosslink density
Thermal expansion coefficient mismatch between core and shell
Volume change of PCM upon phase transition
Interfacial tension between PCM and shell

limitations: HSP values for PCMs change between solid and liquid states; liquid-state values at operating temperature are needed but less commonly tabulated
Crosslinked shell polymers have effectively larger R0 (more solvent-resistant), which modifies RED interpretation
Volume expansion of PCM during melting creates mechanical stress not captured by HSP compatibility alone
Salt hydrate PCMs have very different HSP profiles and may involve ionic interactions beyond HSP framework
Long-term cycling degradation involves chemical aging, not just thermodynamic compatibility
Microencapsulation process kinetics (polymerization rate, emulsion stability) also determine shell quality
known_failure_modes: Paraffin PCMs with low-Tg shell polymers where RED < 0.5 leads to progressive shell softening and leakage over cycling
Salt hydrate PCMs where ionic species interact with shell material through mechanisms not captured by HSP
Shape-stabilized PCMs in porous matrices where capillary effects dominate over HSP-predicted compatibility
High-temperature PCMs (>100C) where polymer shell degradation occurs before HSP-predicted incompatibility
validation_method: DSC cycling tests (1000+ melt-freeze cycles) monitoring latent heat retention
SEM/TEM imaging of capsule morphology before and after cycling
Leakage testing by mass loss measurement on filter paper
Swelling ratio measurement of shell polymer in molten PCM
TGA for thermal stability assessment of encapsulated PCM

key_references: Salaun, F.; Devaux, E.; Bourbigot, S.; Rumeau, P. 'Influence of the Solvent on the Microencapsulation of an Hydrated Salt', Carbohydrate Polymers, 2010, 79, 964-974
Hansen, C.M. 'Hansen Solubility Parameters: A User's Handbook', 2nd Ed., CRC Press, 2007
Su, W.; Darkwa, J.; Kokogiannakis, G. 'Review of Solid-Liquid Phase Change Materials and Their Encapsulation Technologies', Renewable and Sustainable Energy Reviews, 2015, 48, 373-391

Zhang, H.; Wang, X. 'Fabrication and Performances of Microencapsulated Phase Change Materials Based on n-Octadecane Core and Resorcinol-Modified Melamine-Formaldehyde Shell', Colloids and Surfaces A, 2009, 332, 129-138
Jurkowska, M.; Szczygiel, I. 'Review on Properties of Microencapsulated Phase Change Materials Slurries', Applied Thermal Engineering, 2016, 98, 365-373
literature.regulatory_relevance: No direct regulatory framework for HSP-based PCM encapsulation selection. Building energy codes (ASHRAE, EN standards) specify PCM performance requirements but not material selection methodology. For food-contact thermal packaging, FDA and EFSA regulations on polymer shell materials apply indirectly.

industry_sectors: Building and construction (thermal energy storage in walls and ceilings)
Electronics (thermal management of batteries and power electronics)
Solar energy (thermal storage for concentrated solar power)
commercial_tools: Materials Studio (molecular dynamics for interfacial compatibility)
COMSOL Multiphysics (coupled thermal-mechanical modeling of capsules)
Rubitherm (commercial PCM supplier with material datasheets)
Phase Change Energy Solutions (encapsulated PCM product design tools)
